coredata.dc:title: RETRACTED: Iron-iridium metal organic framework/nitrogen doped MXene/graphene quantum dots: A leading composite for electrolytic energy storage and hydrogen evolution reaction

coredata.dc:creator: Zaka, Asma
Iqbal, Muhammad Waqas
Alqorashi, Afaf Khadr
Almutairi, Badriah S.
Alrobei, Hussein
coredata.dc:description:
This article has been retracted: please see Elsevier policy on article withdrawal (https://www.elsevier.com/about/policies-and-standards/article-withdrawal). The Editor in Chief has decided to retract the article. The authors requested extensive changes to the article post-publication which makes the original published findings unreliable. The Editor in Chief thanks the authors for bringing the errors and corrections to the journal's attention. The requested changes are summarized below:
• Revisions to Figs. 1, 2a and 2c, Fig. 3a which included different materials, different labels, changes to certain parameters, and curves.
• A new introduction.
• Added references.

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: Iron-iridium metal organic framework/nitrogen doped MXene/graphene quantum dots: A leading composite for electrolytic energy storage and hydrogen evolution reaction Asma Zaka a, Muhammad Waqas Iqbal a,*, Afaf Khadr Alqorashi b, Badriah S. Almutairi c, Hussein Alrobei d a Department of Physics, Riphah International University, Campus Lahore, Pakistan b Department of Physics, College of Science, Taif University, P.O. Box 11099, Taif, Saudi Arabia c Department of physics, College of Science, Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University, P.O. Box 84428, Riyadh 11671, Saudi Arabia d Department of Mechanical Engineering, College of Engineering, Prince Sattam bin Abdul Aziz University, Al-Kharj 11942, Saudi Arabia A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords: Iron-MOF Iridium-MOF Nitrogen doped MXene Graphene Quantum Dots Power density Energy density and specific capacity A B S T R A C T MXene has recently been the subject of several studies on energy storage. Outstanding qualities that are essential to energy storage applications contain its conductivity, hydrophobicity, and especially significant surface redox reactivity. But while its amazing properties, a few problems seriously restrict its electrochemical efficiency, one of them being the formation of MXene sheets. The gaps between MXene sheets need to be filled with a suitable substance to prevent them from restacking. MOFs have also been thoroughly investigated for electrochemical applications. MOFs have a wide range of uses because of their high crystallinity, porous structure, and large surface area; nevertheless, their effectiveness as electrode materials is restricted by their reduced charging/ discharging rate and specific capacity. In order to overcome these drawbacks, it is suggested that MOFs be combined with appropriate materials for improved performance. This work proposes a novel strategy: a com- posite of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), N-MXene, and ironâ€‘iridium MOF (Ir@Fe-MOF). The limitation of MXene sheet restacking was achieved by reducing each of the ion/electron transportation paths and boosting the electroactive sites, resulting in a remarkable specific capacity in the composite, including MOFs. In a two-electrode assembly, the Ir@Fe-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs exhibited energy and power density of around 43.3 W h kgâˆ’ 1 and 880 W kgâˆ’ 1 respectively. A Coulombic efficiency of 93.06 % and 98.48 % of capacity was maintained by the Ir@Fe-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs/activated carbon (AC) material after 5000 cycles of alternative GCD measurements. The results of this work show that supercapattery applications can gain from the new electrode material Ir@Fe-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs. 1. Introduction A recent study demonstrates a significant increase in global warming and damage to the environment because of more dependency on fossil fuels. The development of safe, sustainable, and highly efficient energy storage technologies to solve environmental protection is one of humanity's most pressing scientific concerns [1â€“3]. Enhancing the effectiveness of energy storage systems has been an important objective in global scientific studies due to its significance in limiting energy usage. In recent years, there have been notable global developments in traditional energy storage technologies, such as batteries and capacitors. [4â€“7]. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are multidimensional and can be modified to specific needs. [8]. Several metal-centered MOFs have been studied regarding potential energy storage uses. The basic elements of MOFs are metal nodes along with organic ligands, which have been thoroughly investigated for their possible use in energy storage. [9â€“11]. Their framework, which is both structurally complex and permeable, can be modified to exhibit different features by adjusting the ratios of organic ligands and metal nodes. [12]. Supercapacitor performance is also significantly improved by the abundance of redox-active sites that MOFs' porous structure offers. Together with other functional materials, MOFs significantly improve conductivity and electrochemical * Corresponding author. E-mail address: waqas.iqbal@riphah.edu.pk (M.W. Iqbal). Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Diamond & Related Materials journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/diamond https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2024.111653 Received 10 May 2024; Received in revised form 19 August 2024; Accepted 7 October 2024 Diamond & Related Materials 149 (2024) 111653 Available online 12 October 2024 0925-9635/Â© 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies. RETRACTED characteristics. [13]. Recently, bimetallic MOFs have been directly carbonized. This has produced synthetic mixed oxides of iron and magnesium that are contained in a nono-porous carbon framework [14]. Iron-MOFs, or Fe-MOFs, have been demonstrated in several studies to be useful as supercapacitors with defined coordination chemistry and a large, rich redox content. One common and abundant metal is iron (Fe) [15]. Organic ligands strongly alter Fe ion electrochemistry, including redox potential and valence states. Many studies focus on organic li- gands since they are important in MOF electrochemistry. As an organic linker, 4,4â€²-bipyridine improves Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs). [19]. 4,4â€™-Bipyridine provides a 3D porous framework for hybrid device electrochemical operations by linking MOF metal sites. The class of transition metal compounds having a metallic structure includes iridium MOF (Ir-MOF), which exhibits metal-like conductivity. It has been applied in the industry for electrical, magnetic, and elec- trochemical applications because of its metal-like conductivity [16,17]. Ir significant pseudocapacitance is one of its unique characteristics [18]. However, in the field of electrochemical capacitor applications, high- surface-area iridium dioxide is seldom investigated in combination with carbonaceous materials. Because organic ligands are essential to MOFs' electrochemical reactions, several studies have given them attention. Benzene-1,3,5-tricarboxylate or 1,4-benzene dicarboxylate moieties (bdc) are employed as an organic linker for MOFs with advanced-level development [19,20]. In order to create a three- dimensional porous structure that is Essential for effective electro- chemical responses in all the hybrid devices, BDC connects the metal nodes in MOFs [21,22]. The numerous active sites as well as porosity in the bimetallic metal-organic framework (MOF) are linked with the remarkable power and energy densities of electrodes in supercapacitors (SCs). These characteristics increase the capacity to store charges and the availability of charge transporters. [23,24]. Mn + 1XnTx represents the two-dimensional substance MXene. In this designation, â€˜X' denotes carbon or nitrogen, â€˜M' is a transition metal from the d-block (such as Ti, Mo, or Cr), and â€˜T' represents a surface functional group such as â€“O, â€“F, or â€“OH) [25,26]. Due to their unique structure, MXenes exhibit remarkable properties such as excellent me- chanical flexibility and electrical conductivity, offering them promising electrode materials for supercapacitors [27,28]. The suitability for many applications can be negatively impacted by flaws in the restacking of nanosheets and very compact aggregation induced by van der Waals forces, as well as limits in MXene surface efficiency and rate perfor- mance [29,30]. Nitrogen effectively changes the surface chemistry and interlayer spacing of MXene, resolving these difficulties [31â€“33]. Graphene quantum dots, which are small graphene nanoparticles that generally measure between 2 and 50 nm, have garnered a lot of attention due to their special properties. These include strong conduc- tivity, enhanced surface area, as well as very high solubility in a variety of solvents [34,35]. Therefore, GQDs have broad applications in a va- riety of fields, such as health, energy conversion, and storage [36,37]. GQDs are an entirely novel type of carbon-based luminous nano- materials [38,39]. GQDs top semiconductor quantum dots due to their superior photostability, chemical inertness, reduced risky effects, high biological compatibility, as well as unique optoelectronic properties. [40,41]. Naturally GQDs have improved surface area as well as interval- to-diameter ratios, among other physical and chemical characteristics [42,43]. This work reveals the application of hybrid heterostructure active electrodes, MOF and N-MXene, and GQDs in high-energy and power- densities, hydrogen evolution reaction and supercapacitors. The ho- mogeneous with appealing progress of active species on the MXene sheets is significantly reduced because of the hydrothermal synthesis facilitation of the two counterparts' synergistic actions. Although hydrogen is an alternative energy source, it is imperative to develop an affordable electrocatalyst for the alkaline hydrogen evolution process (HER). To meet the expanding need for energy storage, a remarkable supercapacitor device is required. The synthesis process for producing active materials supported by MXene has shown impressive electro- chemical performance. The Fe@Ir@MXene hybrid electrode show enhanced electro- chemical properties, attributed to the synergistic effects of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which provide catalytic activity, large surface area, and high electrical conductivity, enabling boosted charge storage capacity, efficient ion diffusion, and prolonged stability, consequently improving performance in hydrogen evolution reactions (HER) and energy storage applications. 2. Experimental section 2.1. Chemicals N, Nâ€²-dimethylformamide (DMF), hydrofluoric acid (HF), ethanol, titanium aluminium carbide (Ti3AlC2), benzene-1,3,5-tricarboxylic (C9H6O6), 1,4-benzene-dicarboxylate moieties (bdc), poly- vinylpyrrolidone (PVDF), and iron(III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3.6H2O) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. 2.2. Iron-iridium MOF synthesis In order to produce Fe@Ir-MOF, deionized water (DIW) was used as the solvent along with Iridium(IV) hexahydrate (Cl6H14IrO6), iron(III) nitrates nonahydrate (Fe(NO3)3.9H2O), and the organic linker tereph- thalic acid (H2BDC). The molar amounts of Iridium(IV) hexahydrate (Cl6H14IrO6) and iron(III) nitrates nonahydrate (Fe(NO3)3.9H2O), that were required to produce the final Fe@Ir-MOF product with the appropriate Fe:Ir ratio were precisely measured. IrO6 (1 M) and Fe (NO3)3 (1 M) were used in equal molar quantities. The metal nitrates were mixed in DIW to ensure complete dissolution. To achieve the required stoichiometry, a unique H2BDC mixture was made in deionized water (DI water) with a constant H2BDC molecular ratio in the entire molar ratio of metal nitrates. Then H2BDC mixture was continuously added to the metal nitrates mixture while constantly stirring it. After stirring for an hour, the resulting mixture was moved to an autoclave that was set to operate at a high temperature of 120 â—¦C in a hydrothermal method [44,45]. After the process was finished, the impurities were extracted by centrifuging the produced Fe@Ir-MOF and cleaning it with DIW. After that, an oven was used to produce Fe@Ir -MOF from the collected material. Fig. 1 shows the preparation of Fe@Ir-MOF using the hydrothermal method. 2.3. Synthesis of MXene and nitrogen-doped MXene Ti2AlC powder was formed by combining titanium carbide, titanium, and aluminium powder in a ball mill for 10 h at a 1:1:1 M ratio. After that, the solution was heated to 1300 â—¦C for an hour at an average of 5 â—¦C per minute in an argon environment [46] The as-prepared Ti2AlC powder was then mixed with an additional molar quantity of titanium carbide powder, ball-milled for a further 10 h, and heated at 1350 â—¦C for 2 h under argon to produce the Ti3AlC2 phase [47,48]. To produce Ti3C2Tx powder, the freshly prepared Ti3AlC2 powder had been sub- jected to HF treatment after 1 h of ball milling. A standard experiment involved stirring 1 g of Ti3AlC2 powder for 3 h at ambient temperature in 20 ml of a 50 % w/w% hydrofluoric acid solution. After repeatedly centrifuging the resulting solution in DI water till pH > 6, it was allowed to dry at room temperature in a vacuum for one to two days. The nitrogen-doped MXenes (N- Ti3C2Tx) were produced by annealing Ti3C2Tx powder in a tube furnace for 2 h at temperature and 100 ml per minute of ammonia gas flow. The material that was produced was designated as N- Ti3C2Tx. 2.4. Synthesis of Fe@Ir-MOF MOF/Nâ€“MXene Fe@Ir-MOF and N-MXene were carefully made separately and then A. Zaka et al. Diamond & Related Materials 149 (2024) 111653 2 RETRACTED combined in a 1:1 ratio to form the nanocomposites mentioned above [49]. By using the distinct qualities of the connected components, this purposeful mixing technique may result in a material with promising features. The N-MXene exhibits great mechanical strength and conduc- tivity, while the Fe@Ir-MOF offers superior porosity and adsorption properties. The hydrogen evolution process and energy storage appli- cations could gain significantly from this synergistic combination. 2.5. Procedure of mixing Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene Obtain the required amount of Fe@Ir-MOF. As an illustration, if a completed composite weighs 100 mg overall, proceed to add 50 mg of Fe@Ir-MOF. Apply ethanol or as a suitable solvent to distribute the Fe@Ir-MOF. In order each 50 mg of Fe@Ir-MOF, add 10 ml in solvent. Sonicate the Fe@Ir-MOF solution for 30 mints for sure a homogenous dispersion. Use a 20â€“30 % volume ultrasonication for your bath / probe. Compute the necessary amount of N-MXene in a similar manner. Thus, 50 milligrammes of N-MXene are needed in a 1:1 ratio. To evenly distribute the N-MXene, apply the same type of solvent which was uti- lized for the Fe@Ir-MOF. Use an extra 10 ml of solvent for every 50 milligrammes of N-MXene. Sonicate it this N-MXene solution for 30 min to achieve a uniform dispersion. Following that, combine the suspen- sions of N-MXene as well as Fe@Ir-MOF into one container. The entire volume for the 1:1 ratio would be around 20 ml. Sonicate it this N- MXene solution for 30 min to achieve a uniform dispersion. Following that, combine the suspensions of N-MXene as well as Fe@Ir-MOF into one container. The entire volume for the 1:1 ratio would be around 20 ml. Centrifuge the entire solution at 10,000 rpm over 10 min. To re- disperse the particles, discard the supernatant while utilizing roughly 5 ml of solvent. The first should briefly sonicate in order to revive the particles. Resume centrifugation to further refine the composite. Finally, use a hoover oven to dry the Fe@Ir-MOF / N-MXene composite for 12 h at 60 â—¦C. Bruker AXS GmbH D8 Advance Eco X-ray diffraction (XRD) had employed to examine the material's structural characteristics. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was conducted using a JEOL JSMâ€“7001F, USA, microscope. Raman spectroscopy was conducted using Tornado's HyperFluxTM PRO Plus, a fully functional system that can be easily integrated into an in-line method. The samples' surface area was measured using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method and a Smartsorb-92/93 surface area analyzer. Using a JPS-9200 and a mono- chromatic Al-Ka X-ray as the energy source, XPS was used to establish the elemental composition of the samples. 3. Results and discussion XRD was used to examine the phase composition and structural analysis of Fe@Ir-MOF, N-MXene, and the Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs nanostructure Fig. 2(a). The Fe@Ir-MOF XRD patterns show that the material is crystalline with clearly defined peak intensities, as well as the absence of any additional diffraction planes confirms the excellent phase purity of the synthesized Fe@Ir-MOF. The phase identification of the Fe- MOF cubic arrangement was verified by the diffraction peaks at 29.37 and 34.25 having lattice points (220) and (310), as well as 25.27 and 41.05 with lattice points (002) and (100) fully complied, respectively [50]. N-MXene's diffraction pattern has a total of four distinct peaks at 7.35, 19.5, 27.19, and 62.23, which match the crystallite planes (002), (004), (006), and (110), accordingly [51]. Additionally, Fig. 2(a) shows Fig. 1. (a) Preparation of Fe@Ir-MOF XRD using hydrothermal method (b) Electrochemical measurements using three electrode assembly. A. Zaka et al. Diamond & Related Materials 149 (2024) 111653 3 RETRACTED the diffraction pattern of the Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs composite. Fe@Ir-MOF combined with N-MXene induces a small shift in the posi- tion at which the peaks emerge. There was an increase in crystallinity from 77.2 % to 82 %. The MOF's insertion into MXene results in compression stress; with numerous displacements, MOF-based ions are dispersed at middle lattice sites. The decrease in layer spacing that is seen in MXene after the MOF is integrated is attributed to the addition of more MOF material planes between the MXene nanocomposite's in- terlayers (Table 1). SEM is utilized to examine the structure and shape of materials. The synthesized materials exhibit a three-dimensional (3D) morphology, according to studies of the samples' basic morphology Fig. 2(b). After being physically mixed with MXene, the obtained Fe@Ir-MOF revealed the morphologies of the doped material. The Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene nanocomposite materials' surface morphological characteristics show how the surface layers of N-MXene and Fe@Ir-MOF are interconnected. The N-MXene phase's mixed nanosheet/flake-like morphology and pores-like structure with a nonuniform pore size distribution were shown by the Fe@Ir-MOF MOF phase results. The resulting arrangement improves ionic diffusion, ionic conductivity, and efficiency applications, as shown by the SEM data. An additional analytical method called Raman spectroscopy can reveal further details about the chemical composition and vibrational modes of the synthesized nanocomposites Fig. 2(c). The presence of the organic linkers 1,4-benzenedicarboxylate and benzene-1,3,5- tricarboxylic in the Fe@Ir-MOF structure is indicated by the peaks at 213 cmâˆ’ 1, 303 cmâˆ’ 1, and 401 cmâˆ’ 1, which match the carboxylate linkages made of metal, Fe@Ir-MOF nanoparticles may be present, as suggested by the vibrational modes while the Fe@Ir metal D band is shown by the peaks at 500, 700 and 938 cmâˆ’ 1. The special peaks detected at the 158, 598, as well as 940 cmâˆ’ 1 were attributed to carbon bending modes and vibrational stretching of Tiâ€“O and Tiâ€“C bonds. As a result, the Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene composite's single pattern displayed all the modes of the separate Fe@Ir-MOF and N-MXene, proving that the mixed composite had formed successfully [52]. The Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs composite's elemental composi- tions as well as surface electronic structure have been determined by X- ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The high-resolution spectra of the Fe 2p area are shown in Fig. 3(a). Through the spin-energy separation of 19.58 eV, the detected peaks at 1025.7 eV and 1045.3 eV are corre- sponding to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, respectively. Moreover, two spin- orbit doublet patterns can be seen in the high-resolution Ir 4f spec- trum for Ir Fig. 3(b). Ir 4f7/2 and Ir 4f5/2 are the identities of the peaks that pair at 62.1 and 65.1 eV, respectively. In Fig. 3(c) showed the C1s spectrum having the binding energy about to 285 eV. Deconvoluted peaks at 457.8, 455.8, and 454.6 eV in Fig. 3(d)â€˜s high-resolution Ti 2p Fig. 2. (a) XRD pattern of Fe@Ir-MOF/GQDs, N-MXene, and Fe@Ir-MOF/ N-MXene/GQDs nanocomposite (b) SEM images Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs nano- composite (c) Raman Spectroscopy of Fe@Ir-MOF/GQDs, N-MXene, and Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs nanocomposite. Table 1 Comparison of stability test for Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs//AC device with literature. Literature No. of cycles Columbic efficiency Capacity retention Reference MgMnS@GQDs 10,000 87.03 % 93 % [75] MnAp@GQDx=75mg 5000 99 % 82 % [76] NiSrS/GQDs-5 % 10,000 100 % 98.3 % [77] MnCuS-GQDs 5000 98 % 89 % [78] AC//AC@GQDs 10,000 99 % 93 % [79] Niâ€“Cu-POx/NGQD 10,000 83 % 86 % [80] GQDs-MoS2/NSG 10,000 93.8 % 92.2 % [81] Fe@Ir-MOF/N- MXene/GQDs 10,000 cycles 93.06 % 98.48 % This Work A. Zaka et al. Diamond & Related Materials 149 (2024) 111653 4 RETRACTED spectra are attributed to Tiâ€“O, Tiâ€“N, as well as Tiâ€“C bonding, accordingly. It is possible to observe the colocalization of pyrrole, N, and Tiâ€“N in Fig. 3(d), which displays the N 1 s spectrum [31]. To evaluate the textural properties and specific surface area of the produced adsorbents, an N2 adsorption desorption study by 77 K is executed to get isotherms Fig. 4(aâ€“c). These isotherms demonstrate that when the P/P0 ratio increases, the N2 uptake levels out after a sharp rise at low P/P0, indicating the occupancy of micropores. A significant hysteresis loop for Fe@Ir-MOF and N-MXene is visible at P/P0 when comparing the isotherms Fig. 4(a) and (b), indicating a battery type material. The Fe@Ir-MOF/Nâ€“MXene/GQDs composite Fig. 4(c) exhibi- ted a hysteretic character, suggesting that the microporosity of the hybrid structure was enhanced by the minute mixing. Fe@Ir-MOF and N-MXene exhibit Brunauerâ€“Emmettâ€“Teller-specific surface areas of 112 as well as 129 m2gâˆ’ 1, respectively. The MOF material contributes to the larger specific surface areas of Fe@Ir-MOF in comparison to N-MXene. Fe@Ir-MOF, N-MXene, and GQDs coupled in situ resulted in specific surface areas of up to 194 m2gâˆ’ 1 Fig. 4(c). The EIS results confirmed the Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs enhanced conductivity, which is significant for supercapacitors and is seen in Fig. 5(a-c). And more significant EIS slope is seen when con- trasting the Fe-MOF/rGO/GQDs electrode with the other three elec- trodes (Fe@Ir-MOF, Nâ€“MXene, along with Fe@Ir-MOF/Nâ€“MXene/ GQDs) [53,54]. The existence of elements like the system's equivalent series resistance (ESR) as well as electrolyte resistance could be responsible for this variation. It is made up of two straight lines: one at low frequencies having a slope larger than 75â—¦ while the other at middle frequencies in the upper frequency range with a 45â—¦ slope. The Fig. 3. (a) XPS spectrum of Fe 2p (b) Ir 4f (c) C1s (d) Nâ€“Ti 2p. Fig. 4. (a) BET analysis spectrum of Fe@Ir-MOF, (b) N-MXene (c) Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs nanocomposite. A. Zaka et al. Diamond & Related Materials 149 (2024) 111653 5 RETRA TED equivalent series resistance (ESR) values for Fe@Ir-MOF, N-MXene and Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs 1.104, 1.211, and 1.094O respectively [55]. 3.1. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) The cyclic voltammetry (CV) results for the Fe@Ir-MOF, N-MXene, and Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs composites are shown in Fig. 6(a-c). Different scan rates were used, ranging from 3 to 50 mV/s, while keeping the potential window constant at 0.75 V. Electrodes are made by covering nickel form using an active substance and operating them in a three-electrode configuration. Hg/HgO and platinum are used for both the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. We dissolved 2.84 g of KOH pellets in 50 ml of DI water to create a 1 M solution of KOH, which we used as an electrolytic solution between these electrodes. The electrochemical behaviour of the active materials is demon- strated by the resulting CV profiles. Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs' improved performance is confirmed by the composite material's much higher specific capacity. All synthetic material's CV curve shows unique redox characteristics, indicating pseudocapacitive behaviour and veri- fying the existence of a redox process. The CV profiles of the Fe@Ir- MOF/N-MXene/GQDs composite material are significant for their amazing consistency, even under extreme conditions with a high scan rate of 50 mV/s. This shows the material's outstanding rate capacity in comparison to other synthesized materials [56â€“59]. This raises the possibility of some limits on reversibility or the rate at Fig. 5. (a) EIS analysis spectrum of Fe@Ir-MOF, (b) N-MXene (c) Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs nanocomposite. Fig. 6. cyclic voltammetry measurements at various scan rates 3 to 50 mV/s (a) Fe@Ir-MOF, (b) N-MXene (c) Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs nanocomposite (d) specific capacity through CV Fe@Ir-MOF, N-MXene, and Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs nanocomposite at different scan rates (e) comparison of specific capacity through CV of all the materials. A. Zaka et al. Diamond & Related Materials 149 (2024) 111653 6 RETRACTED which charge can be stored in the device. An irreversible process is indicated if it is possible to discriminate between the oxidation and reduction peaks on the parallel side. This suggests that charge storage is delayed and that redox processes cause major losses. This knowledge helps determine material compatibility and efficacy. [60]. The Fe@Ir- MOF/N-MXene/GQDs exhibit capacitive behaviour, preventing the material reduction peak often found in publications. [61,62]. The abundance of Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs nanoparticles impeded their participation in the crystallization process of Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/ GQDs. As a result, these nanoparticles have the ability to come together and create networks that allow for the flow of electricity. [63,64]. Fe@Ir-MOF increases conductivity in N-MXene via improving electron and ion mobility through electrostatic absorption/desorption. Electrically charged particles in the material move faster and are unin- hibited by this phenomenon. [65]. The Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQD composites also showed cyclic voltammetry curves having peak cur- rent, indicate the improved charge storage. Charge accumulation by the material determines the extent of these CV curves. [55]. Thus, compared to Fe@Ir-MOF alone, the N-MXene composite has a much larger area, as seen in Fig. 6(b) and (c). When analyzed individ- ually, the graph's more comprehensive section reveals that N-MXene has more capacity and electroactive properties than Fe@Ir-MOFs. Higher scan rates indicate the durability of synthesized material since the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves do not shift much. This consistency highlights the electrodes' durability and efficacy for high-performance energy storage applications by demonstrating their capacity to charge and discharge rapidly. Because of the shorter reaction time between the electrolyte ions and the electrode, there is less contact at the higher scan rates, decreasing specific capacity as well as overall performance. This shorter contact time can result in higher kinetic irreversibility, eventu- ally reducing the energy storage device's efficiency [66]. As shown in Fig. 6(d), this can be shown by comparing the cyclic voltammetry curves for the Fe@Ir-MOF, N-MXene, and Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs com- posites. The combined impact of the composite materials is seen in these curves, which demonstrate an improvement in the electrochemical properties. Pure Fe-MOF has a specific capacity of 513C/g at a scan rate of 3 mV/s. The N-MXene composite has a greater specific capacity of 943C/g under the same conditions. Out of all the samples, the composite material Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs had the most significant specific capacity, reaching 1338C/g at the same 3 mV/s scan rate Fig. 6(d). The Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs composites demonstrated a high specific capacity, this is because of GQDs have large surface area which enhances charge storage, facilitates efficient ion diffusion, and ensure long-term stability, and improving the energy storage performance. Fig. 6(e) comparison of specific capacity through the CV of all the materials (Table 2). 3.2. Galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) Using the galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) technique, the electrodes for Fe@Ir-MOF, N-MXene, and Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs were tested for their capacity to store charge. The electrochemical properties of the Fe@Ir-MOF, N-MXene, and Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/ GQDs are shown in Fig. 7(a-c) as a galvanostatic charge/discharge research graph. Fe@Ir-MOF electrode was shown to have a somewhat poor specific capacity after examination. [67]. GCD was measured in N- MXene and Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQD composites. At 0.6 V, current densities ranged from 1 to 2.6 A/g. Fig. 7(b) and 7(c) show Fe@Ir-MOF/ N-MXene/GQDs and N-MXene GCD curves. The findings demonstrated that the duration of the discharging phase reduced in proportion to the rise in current density. The GCD curves of Fe@Ir-MOF exhibited the anticipated standard characteristics when current densities were varied. The composites of N-MXene and Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs had higher discharge peaks than Fe@Ir-MOF. The combined impact of Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs increases electrical charge storage and discharge peak. Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs improve charge conduc- tion, increasing the discharging peak. The GCD measurements confirm the CV findings and show that the Fe@Ir-MOF, N-MXene, and Fe@Ir- MOF/N-MXene/GQDs electrodes have battery-like properties. The GCD curves for Fe@Ir-MOF, N-MXene, and Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/ GQDs composites are compared in Fig. 7(d). These graphs, which clearly show an improvement in the electrochemical properties, demonstrate the cumulative influence of the composite materials. Pure Fe-MOF has a 513C/g specific capacity at a 1 A/g scan rate. N-MXene has a greater specific capacity of 943 A/g under similar conditions. The Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs has the highest specific capacity, 1338C/g at 1 A/g Fig. 7(d). Increased surface area and remarkable electroactivity of the Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQD composites lead to more active sites for effective charge storage. Fig. 7(e) employs cyclic voltammetry (CV) to compare the specific capacity of every material. The GCD results support the earlier CV results and imply that Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/ GQDs have great promise for use in supercapattery systems. 3.3. Supercapattery assembly Asymmetric two-electrode analysis was performed on the Fe@Ir- MOF/N-MXene/GQDs doped material electrode. Fig. 8(a) shows the supercapattery two-electrode assembly design. The activated carbon negative electrode and Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs positive electrode were compared. A nickel foam current collector was coated with the Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs composite, and a porous membrane was kept separate from the activated carbon cathode. Because of its high porosity and conductivity, activated carbon (AC) was especially chosen to serve as the device's negative electrode. This special combination makes improved ion absorption with in the electrolyte possible [68]. Fig. 8(b) uses cyclic voltammetry (CV) at different scan rates (3 to 100 mV/s) to assess the electrochemical performance of the supercapattery device using Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs. The quasi-rectangular pat- terns were displayed by the supercapattery CV peaks, however, when the potentials increased the resulting shapes became becoming dis- torted. This effect likely comes due to the device's two different energy storage methods, resulting in various electrochemical processes. The non-ideal shape of the cyclic voltammogram curves, especially at upper scan rates, is caused by restricted mobility of ions during the redox processes on the electrode surface. Because of this limitation, active sites cannot be utilized to their maximum potential, which results in shapes other than rectangles. The hybrid device that combines the advantages of supercapacitors and batteries has a defined b-value between 0.5 and 0.8. The device's b-values have been shown in Fig. 8(c), confirm the classification of this device as a supercapattery by highlighting its distinct features and reliability. As demonstrated in Fig. 8(c), the b- values of the Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs//AC supercapattery device suggest that it may be classified as a supercapattery. A certain b-value between 0.5 and 0.8 characterizes the device having both the features of batteries and supercapacitors. This work demonstrates the Fe@Ir-MOF/ N-MXene/GQDs//AC device's special charge storage features, making it appropriate for a broad range of energy storage applications. [69]. The cyclic voltammogram curves of the supercapattery device using Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs show remarkable rate capability even at Table 2 Showed comparison of energy and power densities for Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/ GQDs. Materials Power density Energy density References Multi-wall CNT cobalt- oxideâ€‘silver 298 W kgâˆ’ 1 17 Wh kgâˆ’ 1 [82] Cobalt hydroxide 300 W kgâˆ’ 1 10 Wh kgâˆ’ 1 [83] Cobalt nickel silicon 95 W kgâˆ’ 1 20 Wh kgâˆ’ 1 [84] Manganese cobalt oxide 319 W kgâˆ’ 1 34 Wh kgâˆ’ 1 [85] Manganese iron oxide 290 W kgâˆ’ 1 27 Wh kgâˆ’ 1 [86] Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs 880 W kgâˆ’ 1 43 Wh kgâˆ’ 1 This Work A. Zaka et al. Diamond & Related Materials 149 (2024) 111653 7 RETRACTED higher scan rates, suggesting it. This device is called a supercapattery. The peaks seen in the cyclic voltammogram exhibited quasi-rectangular shapes, which grew increasingly distorted as the potentials increased. The restraint on ion transport through the electrode surface electro- chemical oxidation-reduction process causes the CV curves to change from rectangular to non-rectangular at higher scan rates. This constraint limits active site use, resulting in an inefficient form. The GCD (galva- nostatic charge-discharge) curves for the hybrid device that was pro- duced using Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs are displayed in Fig. 8(d). These GCD curves have an unusual form that combines humped and triangular profiles, which is like that was observed in the cyclic vol- tammetry study. Fig. 8d displays the charging-discharging (GCD) curves for the supercapattery device (Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs) at various current densities. Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs have been utilized by the supercapattery device to maintain a steady potential range of 0 to 1.6 V. Performance was constant, as can be seen by the apparent reliability of the charging-discharging curves generated at various current den- sities. Reduction in total resistance is shown by the lowest voltage drop across the internal resistance (IR). The formula, which comes from the GCD graphs, was applied to ascertain the capacity of the issue. [70]. Q = 2 Ã— I Ã— Î”t m (1) Eq. (1) calculates the specific capacity by considering the mass of active material on both substrates (m), discharge current (I), and dis- charging duration (t). Fig. 9(a) and (b) shown the precise capacity ac- quired by CV and galvanostatic GCD of the real device, respectively. A specific capacity of 126C/g was shown by the supercapattery device (Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs//AC) using CV analysis at a scan rate of 3 mV/s. Further, the GCD study revealed that, at a current density of 1 A/ g, the Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs//AC combination showed a specific capacity of 200C/g. An energy storage device's reduced capacity follows the current density increase. The short duration is the main cause of this decrease in the interaction between the ion and the electrode material. The cycle life, which refers to the capacity of supercapattery devices to maintain performance across several charge-discharge cycles, is an important factor to consider. Fig. 9(c) shows that the maximum columbic efficiency achieved throughout these cycles is 93 %. Further- more, 98 % of the capacity could be maintained after 1000 cycles. The Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs//AC supercapattery device demonstrated outstanding cycle stability and capacity storage. Higher energy density means the device can store more energy. The following formulas calculated energy and power density. E = Q Ã— Î”V 2 Ã— 3.6 (2) P = E Ã— 3600 Î”t (3) The equations use variables such as Î”V (potential window), Q (specific capacity), E (energy density), Î”t (discharge time), and P (power density). The specific energy of the supercapattery device (Fe@Ir-MOF/N- MXene/GQDs//AC) is 43 Wh kgâˆ’ 1, and its power is 880 W kgâˆ’ 1. This data illustrates the rates at which the device stores and releases energy. Fig. 9(d) compares the results achieved in this investigation and those found in the literature. Fig. 7. (a) Galvanostatic charging/discharging measurements at various current densities 1 to 2.6 A/g (a) Fe@Ir-MOF, (b) N-MXene (c) Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs nanocomposite (d) specific capacity through GCD Fe@Ir-MOF, N-MXene, and Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs nanocomposite at different scan rates (e) comparison of specific capacity through GCD of all the materials. A. Zaka et al. Diamond & Related Materials 149 (2024) 111653 8 RETRACTED 3.4. Hydrogen evolution reaction The HER of Fe@Ir-MOF, N-MXene, and Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs nanocomposites was determined using a typical three-electrode arrangement [71]. The efficacy of the coated electrodes was evaluated in the HER experiment against the most effective Pt/C reference elec- trode and the properties and performance of platinum carbon is better than material and we used the platinum carbon as a reference electrode [72]. The Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs coated electrode exhibits the highest HER catalytic activity among the composites however it is lower than the Pt/C-decorated electrode. This is demonstrated by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves [73,74] Fig. 10(a). Pt/C, Fe@Ir-MOF/N- MXene/GQDs, N-MXene and Fe@Ir-MOF overpotentials are summa- rized as 133, 175, 199, and 150 mV, accordingly, in Fig. 10(b). This indicates that the Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs binary nanocomposite works better since it needs a far lower overpotential to attain equiv.âˆ’ 1). The current densities result from an H+ attaching to the catalyst's surface during the early phases of the HER. The Fe@Ir-MOF/Nâ€“MXene/GQDs composite exhibits a lower Tafel slope (38.54 mV decâˆ’ 1) compared to Fe@Ir-MOF (43.87 mV decâˆ’ 1) and Nâ€“MXene (61.62 mV decâˆ’ 1) when hydrogen is adsorbed. Eq. (4) indicates that the procedure is known as the Volmer step or the discharge step. Then, as stated in Eq. (5), a H* combines with an H+ and an electron (eâˆ’ ) to form an H2. On the other hand, H2 can be formed by the Tafel step, which is also known as the process of chemical desorption step. This requires the alignment of two H* atoms on the catalyst's surface, as demonstrated by Eq. (6). The whole reaction of the HER is displayed in Eq. (7), where the standard electrode potential (E1), which is used to quantify the standard elec- trode potential of electrochemical reactions, is identified. H+ + eâˆ’ â†’H* (4) H* +H+ + eâˆ’ â†’H2 (5) 2H*â†’H2 (6) 2H* +2eâˆ’ â†’H2 (7) In addition, compared to this research, the Pt/C has an optimal value Fig. 10(c). It is essential to determine the stability of an electrocatalyst, which can be done by analyzing CV for 5000 cycles at 2 mV/s. Fig. 10(d) explains the long-term sustainability of the sample with a slight fluctu- ation in the LSV curve before and after 1000 cycles. The Fe@Ir-MOF nanocomposite's structure and crystal phase stability are responsible for its exceptional stability. 4. Conclusion Our first approach to designing the Fe@Ir-MOF/Nâ€“MXene/GQDs composite was successful for energy storage and hydrogen evolution reaction applications. The main objective is to increase supercapacitors' energy density by using different electrolytes and innovative electrode materials. The results from the x-ray diffraction (XRD) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) of the surface confirmed the successful synthesis of the Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs composite. The composite exhibited a crystallite size in the nanometer range. Using the GCD curve, Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs were able to attain a specific capacity of 1648 Cgâˆ’ 1. The supercapattery (Fe@Ir-MOF/Nâ€“MXene/GQDs) demon- strated an energy density of 43 W h kgâˆ’ 1 under the electrolyte and a Fig. 8. (a) Systematic illustration of two electrode assembly for supercapattery (b) CV measurements of Fe@Ir-MOF/GQDs/N-GQDs//AC at different scan rates (c) b- fitting value graph of Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs//AC (d) GCD measurements of Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs//AC at different current densities. A. Zaka et al. Diamond & Related Materials 149 (2024) 111653 9 RETRACTED Fig. 9. (a) Specific capacities of Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs//AC, /N-MXene//AC, and Fe@Ir-MOF//AC through CV measurements at different scan rates (b) (a) Specific capacities of Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs//AC, /N-MXene//AC, and Fe@Ir-MOF//AC through GCD measurements at different current densities (c) capacity retention of Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs//AC (d) Energy and power density of Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs//AC. Fig. 10. (a) Linear sweep Voltammetry for Fe@Ir-MOF, Pt/C, N-MXene, and Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs (b) Overpotential graph for Fe@Ir-MOF, Pt/C, N-MXene, and Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs (c) Tafel slope for Fe@Ir-MOF, Pt/C, N-MXene, and Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs (d) LSV of Fe@Ir-MOF/N-MXene/GQDs before and after 1000 cycles. A. Zaka et al. Diamond & Related Materials 149 (2024) 111653 10 RETRACTED power density of 880 W kgâˆ’ 1. The supercapattery (Fe@Ir-MOF/ Nâ€“MXene/GQDs) retained 98 % of its capacity and 93 % of its Coulombic efficiency after 5000 cycles. Because of the Fe@Ir-MOF/ Nâ€“MXene/GQDs composite, the HER has a reduced potential barrier, measuring 38.54 mV decâˆ’ 1 with a 150 mV overpotential at âˆ’ 10 mA cmâˆ’ 2. This new technique makes mixing pristine MOFs with MXenes in supercapacitor applications easier, which also has the potential for mixed metal MOF and MXene composite applications in other electro- chemical devices. CRediT authorship contribution statement Asma Zaka: Writing â€“ original draft. Muhammad Waqas Iqbal: Supervision. Afaf Khadr Alqorashi: Formal analysis. Badriah S. Almutairi: Data curation. Hussein Alrobei: Writing â€“ review & editing. Declaration of competing interest The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper. Data availability Upon request, the data can be made available. Acknowledgements The researchers would like to acknowledge Deanship of Scientific Research, Taif University for funding this work. head.ce:title: RETRACTED: Iron-iridium metal organic framework/nitrogen doped MXene/graphene quantum dots: A leading composite for electrolytic energy storage and hydrogen evolution reaction

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This article has been retracted: please see Elsevier policy on article withdrawal (https://www.elsevier.com/about/policies-and-standards/article-withdrawal).

The Editor in Chief has decided to retract the article. The authors requested extensive changes to the article post-publication which makes the original published findings unreliable. The Editor in Chief thanks the authors for bringing the errors and corrections to the journal's attention. The requested changes are summarized below:
•
Revisions to Figs. 1, 2a and 2c, Fig. 3a which included different materials, different labels, changes to certain parameters, and curves.
•
A new introduction.
•
Added references.
